set.description: Reads or writes the value of a variable. If value is specified, sets the variable to that value and returns it; otherwise returns the variable's current value. This is the primary way to create or modify variables in Tcl.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to read or set.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: Optional. The value to assign to the variable. If omitted, the current value is returned.

set.example:
set voltage 1.2
set current $voltage
puts $current  ;# prints: 1.2

append.description: Appends all of the value arguments to the current value of the variable named by varName. If the variable does not exist, it is created. This is efficient for building large strings incrementally.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to append to. Created if it does not exist.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: One or more string values to append to the variable.

append.example:
set result "Mesh: "
append result "region1" " " "region2"
puts $result  ;# prints: Mesh: region1 region2

lappend.description: Appends each value argument as a separate list element to the variable named by varName. If the variable does not exist, it is created as a list. Treats the variable's current value as a list.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to append list elements to. Created if it does not exist.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: One or more values to append as individual list elements.

incr.description: Increments the value of a variable by the given amount. The variable must contain an integer string. If increment is omitted, it defaults to 1. Use a negative increment to decrement.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable holding an integer value.

parameters.name: increment
parameters.desc: Optional. The amount to add (default 1). Can be negative.

incr.example:
set count 0
incr count       ;# count is now 1
incr count 5     ;# count is now 6
incr count -2    ;# count is now 4

foreach.signature: foreach varname list body ?varlist2 list2 ...? body
foreach.description: Iterates over one or more lists, assigning elements to variables and executing the body for each set of elements. Multiple lists can be traversed in parallel; the loop continues until all lists are exhausted.

parameters.name: varname
parameters.desc: Variable to receive the current list element.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list to iterate over.

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: Script to execute for each element.

lmap.signature: lmap varname list body ?varlist2 list2 ...? body
lmap.description: Maps a script over list elements and collects the results into a new list. Like foreach, but returns the collected results. Supports multiple list traversal. Tcl 8.6+.

parameters.name: varname
parameters.desc: Variable to receive the current element.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list to map over.

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: Script whose result becomes the corresponding element of the output list.

lmap.example:
set doubled [lmap v {1 2 3} {expr {$v * 2}}]  ;# returns {2 4 6}
set squares [lmap x {0 1 2 3 4} {expr {$x * $x}}]  ;# returns {0 1 4 9 16}

lassign.description: Assigns list elements to the specified variables in order. Returns the remaining (unassigned) elements. This is a convenient way to unpack a list into named variables. Tcl 8.5+.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list whose elements to assign.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Variable names to receive list elements in order.

gets.description: Reads a single line from a channel. If varName is specified, stores the line in that variable and returns the number of characters read (-1 at end-of-file). If varName is omitted, returns the line itself.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: The channel to read from.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the line. Returns character count instead of the line itself.

scan.description: Parses a string according to a format string (like C sscanf). Extracts values from the input string and optionally stores them in the named variables. Returns the number of successfully matched items.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The input string to parse.

parameters.name: formatString
parameters.desc: C-style format specifiers (%d, %f, %s, etc.) describing the expected input format.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable names to store the parsed values.

scan.example:
scan "Vg=1.2 Vd=0.5" "Vg=%f Vd=%f" vg vd
puts "Gate: $vg, Drain: $vd"  ;# prints: Gate: 1.2, Drain: 0.5

regexp.description: Matches a regular expression against a string. Returns 1 if a match is found, 0 otherwise. If match variables are specified, they are set to the matched text and any captured subexpressions. Supports standard regex syntax.

parameters.name: -nocase
parameters.desc: Optional. Case-insensitive matching.

parameters.name: -all
parameters.desc: Optional. Match as many times as possible.

parameters.name: -inline
parameters.desc: Optional. Return the match data as a list instead of setting variables.

parameters.name: -line
parameters.desc: Optional. Make ^ and $ match beginning/end of each line, not the whole string.

parameters.name: exp
parameters.desc: The regular expression pattern.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The string to search.

parameters.name: matchVar
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable to store the full match.

parameters.name: subMatchVar
parameters.desc: Optional. Variables to store captured subexpression matches.

regsub.description: Performs regular expression substitution on a string. Replaces the first (or all with -all) match of the pattern with the substitution specification. Returns the number of matches found.

parameters.name: -all
parameters.desc: Optional. Replace all occurrences, not just the first.

parameters.name: -nocase
parameters.desc: Optional. Case-insensitive matching.

parameters.name: exp
parameters.desc: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The input string to process.

parameters.name: subSpec
parameters.desc: The replacement string. Use & for the matched text, \1-\9 for submatches.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable to store the result. If omitted, the result is returned.

regsub.example:
set path "/home/user/sim/run1_des.cmd"
regsub -all {/} $path "\\" winpath
puts $winpath  ;# prints: \home\user\sim\run1_des.cmd

catch.description: Catches exceptions from the script. Returns 0 if the script completes normally, or 1 (or other error codes) if an error occurred. Optionally stores the result or error message and error options in the named variables.

parameters.name: script
parameters.desc: The Tcl script to execute and monitor for errors.

parameters.name: resultVarName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the script's return value or error message.

parameters.name: optionsVarName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the error options dictionary (error code, stack trace, etc.).

variable.description: Creates and optionally sets one or more namespace variables. Typically used inside a namespace eval block to define variables that belong to that namespace. Each name-value pair sets the variable; a name without a value declares it without initialization.

parameters.name: name
parameters.desc: Variable name to create or set within the current namespace.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: Optional. Value to assign to the variable.

global.description: Makes one or more global variables accessible within a procedure scope. Without this command, procedures have their own local scope and cannot directly access global variables.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: One or more global variable names to access inside a procedure.

upvar.description: Creates a local variable (myVar) that is linked to a variable in a different scope (otherVar). This allows procedures to modify variables in the caller's scope, similar to pass-by-reference in other languages.

parameters.name: level
parameters.desc: Optional. The scope level (default 1, meaning the caller's scope). Use #0 for global scope.

parameters.name: otherVar
parameters.desc: The variable name in the target scope to link to.

parameters.name: myVar
parameters.desc: The local variable name that will be linked to otherVar.

upvar.example:
proc double_value {varName} {
  upvar 1 $varName var
  set var [expr {$var * 2}]
}
set x 5
double_value x
puts $x  ;# prints: 10
